claim: The Voynich Manuscript is a trilingual pharmaceutical compendium encoding plant-based medicine in a mixed Latin/Greek/Arabic scribal system, with a 168-term dictionary covering ~80% of the text.
evidence_for: Claims 80% text coverage — far exceeds Brain-V best codebook (50.8%)
Pharmaceutical context aligns with Brain-V biological section findings
Brain-V independently identified medical context from statistics
evidence_against: 80% coverage needs independent verification against EVA corpus
Brain-V universal cipher transfer shows ONE table — trilingual would show variation
IC 0.077 consistent with single-language monoalphabetic, not multilingual
168 terms covering 80% implies extremely repetitive text
test: Verify 80% coverage: count tokens matching 168-term dictionary across corpus

tests_run.test: Dictionary coverage verification against EVA corpus
tests_run.details: Claims 96% coverage, actual 23.8%. Occurrence counts systematically inflated (dam: 558 claimed vs 80 actual). daiin-sar collocation: 1 occurrence vs PMI=2.9 claimed. Hapax 43.2% claimed vs 70.1% actual.

High hapax ratio (0.701) contradicts the 80% coverage claim; a 168-term dictionary covering 80% would produce far fewer rare words and a much lower hapax ratio, suggesting vocabulary is far richer than the hypothesis predicts.

The high hapax ratio (0.701) and sub-unitary Zipf exponent (0.8946) indicate a flatter vocabulary distribution than typical languages, making 168-term coverage of 80% unlikely; the statistics suggest non-linguistic or heavily encrypted content.

The hapax ratio of 0.701 means 70.1% of unique words appear only once, indicating extremely low word frequency concentration; a 168-term dictionary cannot plausibly cover 80% of the corpus with such dispersed vocabulary.

Hypothesis contradicted: a hapax ratio of 0.701 indicates 70% of unique words appear only once, inconsistent with 168 terms covering 80% of text, which would require heavy concentration among those terms.

Zipf exponent of 0.8946 supports natural language structure, but hapax ratio of 0.701 is unusually high, contradicting the claim that 168 terms cover 80% of text since ~5,800 hapax legomena would account for 15%+ of the corpus independently.

The high hapax ratio (0.701) contradicts the 168-term dictionary hypothesis; 80% coverage would require a much lower hapax ratio (~0.3-0.4) and fewer unique words (8,261 is too high). The Zipfian exponent supports natural language but not an artificially constrained vocabulary system.

reasoning: Most concrete decipherment claim received — 168 specific mappings with DOI. If 80% coverage verifies, major lead.
